chunk_summary: This section discusses diagnostic endoscopy and histologic thresholds for eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE), focusing on food allergy-related therapeutic elimination diets and pediatric management guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of endoscopy in diagnosing eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE).
Identify histologic thresholds for EoE diagnosis, including eosinophil counts.
Explore the relationship between food allergies and therapeutic elimination diets in EoE management.
Review pediatric-specific guidelines for EoE diagnosis and treatment.
key_facts: Eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE) diagnosis requires ≥15 eosinophils per high-power field (HPF) on esophageal biopsy.
Endoscopy findings in EoE may include rings, furrows, and white exudates in the esophagus.
Therapeutic elimination diets are effective in managing EoE, with up to 70% of patients responding to a six-food elimination diet (SFED).
Pediatric EoE guidelines emphasize the importance of histologic confirmation and dietary management.
EoE is often associated with food allergies, with milk, wheat, soy, and eggs being common triggers.
reference_quote: Eosinophilic esophagitis diagnosis in children requires histologic confirmation of ≥15 eosinophils per high-power field (HPF) on esophageal biopsy, as per ESPGHAN guidelines (2024).
